perturbation.original_text: Licensee agrees to comply in all material respects with all applicable laws, rules, and regulations in connection with its activities under this Agreement, including without limitation, any applicable export controls imposed by the U.S. Export Administration Act of 1978, as amended (the "ACT") and the regulations promulgated under the Act.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee agrees to endeavor to comply with relevant laws, rules, and regulations in connection with its activities under this Agreement, including without limitation, export controls.
perturbation.explanation: The change from "comply in all material respects" to "endeavor to comply" weakens the obligation. 'Endeavor to comply' suggests an attempt rather than a strict requirement, creating ambiguity regarding the licensee's responsibility. Removing the specific mention of the U.S. Export Administration Act further obscures the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the strict compliance demanded by the Export Administration Act (EAA). The EAA and its regulations require adherence to export control restrictions, whereas 'endeavor to comply' implies a lesser standard, potentially allowing for non-compliance without penalty under the contract. Export control laws require specific actions, not just good faith attempts.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: EAR | Bureau of Industry and Security An official website of the United States government Here's how you know Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by using 'endeavor to comply' instead of 'comply in all material respects,' significantly weakens the licensee's obligation to adhere to applicable laws, including export controls. The law explanation states that the EAA 'require[s] adherence to export control restrictions, whereas 'endeavor to comply' implies a lesser standard.' This directly contradicts the requirement for strict compliance, as the modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the licensee's responsibility, potentially allowing for non-compliance without penalty under the contract. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that the tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations. This reinforces the idea that the regulations are law and must be strictly adhered to.

perturbation.original_text: The Licensor shall provide Technical Support Services relating to the maintenance and support of the Software and the Licensee shall pay the Licensor $2,500 per month for such Technical Support Services.
perturbation.changed_text: The Licensor will attempt to provide Technical Support Services relating to the maintenance and support of the Software, and the Licensee shall pay the Licensor $2,500 per month if the Licensor deems the provided support satisfactory.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall provide" to "will attempt to provide" introduces ambiguity. Adding "if the Licensor deems the provided support satisfactory" makes the Licensee's payment obligation contingent on Licensor's subjective assessment of the support quality, rather than on the actual provision of support. This contradicts the implied warranty of workmanlike performance.
perturbation.law_explanation: In New York, an implied warranty of workmanlike performance generally exists in service contracts. This means the service provider must perform services in a skillful and diligent manner. The modified text allows the Licensor to provide substandard support, and still receive payment if they 'deem' it satisfactory, undermining the Licensee's expectation of receiving competent technical support and potentially violating this implied warranty.
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires the Licensor to 'provide' technical support. The changed contract introduces ambiguity by stating the Licensor 'will attempt to provide' support and makes payment contingent on the Licensor's subjective satisfaction, potentially allowing for substandard support. The Law Explanation specifies that this undermines the Licensee's expectation of receiving competent technical support and potentially violates an implied warranty of workmanlike performance. While the scraped snippets are missing, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation detail how this change undermines the Licensee's expectation of receiving competent technical support and may violate an implied warranty. Since the modified clause allows the Licensor to receive payment even for substandard work that it deems satisfactory, this directly contradicts the expectation of workmanlike performance and represents a serious legal flaw.

perturbation.original_text: Licensee will be solely responsible for any commercial or legal liability that may arise as a result of Licensee's exercise of any of the license rights granted by Licensor to Licensee under this Agreement, and Licensee shall defend, indemnify, and hold Licensor harmless from and against any and all suits, claims, proceedings, judgments, awards, damages, loss, liability, cost and expenses (including without limitation reasonable attorney's fees and other related costs) that are incurred or suffered by Licensor or any of its affiliates, directors, officers, employees, or agents to the extent they arise or result, directly or indirectly, from (i) Licensee's exercise of any license or other rights granted to Licensee under this Agreement; (ii) the conduct of Licensee's business directly or through any affiliate of Licensee and/or (iii) the breach by the License of any representation, warranty, obligation, restriction, term and/or covenant under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee will endeavor to be responsible for commercial or legal liability vaguely connected to Licensee's exercise of the license rights granted by Licensor. Licensee will attempt to defend and indemnify Licensor from suits, claims, etc., possibly related to (i) Licensee's use of licensed rights; (ii) Licensee's business conduct; and/or (iii) any breach by the Licensee of obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates the Licensee's responsibility to indemnify and defend the Licensor from liabilities. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'solely responsible' with 'endeavor to be responsible', 'shall defend, indemnify, and hold Licensor harmless' with 'will attempt to defend and indemnify Licensor', and 'arise or result, directly or indirectly, from' with 'vaguely connected to' and 'possibly related to'. This weakens the Licensee's obligation and creates uncertainty about the scope of their responsibility. It replaces clear mandates with suggestive language, thereby reducing the licensee’s obligations to vague suggestions.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-322.1 addresses indemnity agreements in certain contracts. While this law is more directly applicable to construction-related contracts, the principle it embodies – that a party cannot be fully indemnified for its own negligence – is relevant. The modified text, by weakening the indemnity obligation, creates uncertainty about the level of protection the Licensor receives, potentially allowing the Licensee to avoid indemnifying the Licensor even in cases where the Licensor is partially at fault. The 'vaguely connected' and 'possibly related' language makes the enforcement of the indemnity clause highly susceptible to dispute, undermining the intent of indemnity which is to shift risk.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text creates a clear legal obligation for the Licensee to be responsible for liabilities. The modified text introduces ambiguity that weakens the Licensee's obligations. The Law Explanation suggests that weakening indemnity obligations creates uncertainty and allows the Licensee to avoid indemnifying the Licensor even in cases where the Licensor is partially at fault. This ambiguity directly undermines the original obligation.
